Citations in Scholarly Publishing

Scholarly articles weave evidence into prose through footnote markers that point at a separate list of references. When such an article is converted to Markdown for downstream consumption, preserving the link between a citation marker and its definition keeps the citation structure intact instead of collapsing it into undifferentiated text.

The practice dates back centuries1 and continues today across academic publishers, encyclopedias, and technical documentation. Modern HTML encodes the link structurally: a superscript anchor pointing at a definition list item by fragment identifier.

Wikipedia popularized a slightly different convention[1], using cite_ref and cite_note identifiers and rendering a back-to-text caret alongside each definition.

A second source reinforces the structural argument2 and is included so the rendered output exercises multi-entry numbering.

Regardless of the convention, a converter that recognizes both halves of the pairing can re-emit the citation as a Markdown footnote: an inline [^N] marker plus a trailing definitions block. Articles with no footnotes are passed through untouched, so the conversion stays free of false positives.

References

  1. Grafton, Anthony. The Footnote: A Curious History. Harvard University Press, 1999.
  2. A second definition included so the rendering exercises multi-entry output.
  1. ^ Wikipedia contributors. "Wikipedia:Citing sources."
